verses.text: Amaziah of Judah does right, but not like David, and the high places remain. His obedience is real yet incomplete. A faith that pleases God in much can still leave standing the compromises it never quite dares to remove.

Amaziah of Judah does right, but not like David, and the high places remain. His obedience is real yet incomplete. A faith that pleases God in much can still leave standing the compromises it never quite dares to remove.

Amaziah of Judah does right, but not like David, and the high places remain. His obedience is real yet incomplete. A faith that pleases God in much can still leave standing the compromises it never quite dares to remove.

Amaziah of Judah does right, but not like David, and the high places remain. His obedience is real yet incomplete. A faith that pleases God in much can still leave standing the compromises it never quite dares to remove.

Once secure, he executes his father's murderers, but spares their children, obeying the law that each shall die for his own sin. He shows restraint where revenge was easy. To keep God's word even against the pull of grief and grievance is the mark of true reverence.

Once secure, he executes his father's murderers, but spares their children, obeying the law that each shall die for his own sin. He shows restraint where revenge was easy. To keep God's word even against the pull of grief and grievance is the mark of true reverence.

Amaziah strikes down ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt and takes their stronghold. God grants him a real victory. Yet, as what follows shows, success can swell a heart toward pride as quickly as it confirms God's blessing.

Flushed with victory, Amaziah challenges Jehoash of Israel, who answers with the parable of the thistle that thought itself a cedar: be content with your glory, and stay home. Pride is warned to know its limits. The puffed-up heart courts a fall it cannot see coming.

Flushed with victory, Amaziah challenges Jehoash of Israel, who answers with the parable of the thistle that thought itself a cedar: be content with your glory, and stay home. Pride is warned to know its limits. The puffed-up heart courts a fall it cannot see coming.

Flushed with victory, Amaziah challenges Jehoash of Israel, who answers with the parable of the thistle that thought itself a cedar: be content with your glory, and stay home. Pride is warned to know its limits. The puffed-up heart courts a fall it cannot see coming.

Amaziah will not listen, and Judah is routed; Jerusalem's wall is broken and the temple plundered. The proud challenge ends in humiliation. Refusing wise counsel, a man drags his people into the disaster his own arrogance invited.

Jehoash of Israel dies and is buried in Samaria, and his son reigns. The northern line continues its course. The chronicler moves steadily on, noting how kingdom after kingdom rises and passes under God's overruling hand.

Jehoash of Israel dies and is buried in Samaria, and his son reigns. The northern line continues its course. The chronicler moves steadily on, noting how kingdom after kingdom rises and passes under God's overruling hand.

Amaziah lives on, but a conspiracy finally hunts him down and kills him at Lachish; his body is brought back on horses. The reign that started well ends in flight and murder. Pride unrepented hollows out a life until it falls to those who once served it.

The people make young Azariah king in his father's place, and he rebuilds Elath for Judah. A new reign begins with promise. After the failures of the fathers, God still raises up sons to carry the kingdom forward.

The people make young Azariah king in his father's place, and he rebuilds Elath for Judah. A new reign begins with promise. After the failures of the fathers, God still raises up sons to carry the kingdom forward.

Jeroboam II of Israel does evil, yet God uses him to restore Israel's borders, for the LORD saw their bitter affliction and had not said He would blot them out. Mercy flows even to an undeserving king for the people's sake. His compassion delays a judgment justice could demand.

Jeroboam reigns mightily, then dies, and his son Zachariah succeeds him. The strong reign passes like all the rest. Outward greatness without true repentance only postpones the reckoning that the prophets had long foretold.
